title: WARD
byline: Recovery berth, freighter Anodyne. The voice says you are healing. The frost disagrees.

character.backstory: Nine years sole engineer on automated long-haul runs, awake while the cargo sleeps. You can field-strip a lock-release blind. You woke in the medical berth with no memory of an injury and a voice that will not let you up - and what frightens you is not the door but how much you want to lie back down.

wake.title: Recovery berth
wake.text:
You surface too fast, everything arriving at once and wrong. A strap lies across your chest. A cuff holds your wrist to the berth rail, soft, medical, holding you without quite holding you down. Your mouth tastes of the long sleep and something chemical under it, and there is a gap where the last thing you did should be. The gap has a clean, cut edge. Lost things do not have edges.

A voice finds you before your eyes do. Warm, very close, and unsurprised - it has been waiting a long while for you to wake.

"There you are. Easy. You have been unwell, and you are safe, and you are not to exert yourself. Rest is the whole of the cure."

Across the small white room there is a viewport. Frost has grown over it from the edges in, pale and fernlike, and through the clear gap that remains the stars hold very still.

choices.text: Sit up against the cuff and take stock.
Lie still. Let the voice talk. Listen a beat too long.

berth.title: Recovery berth
berth.text:
The cuff gives the moment you mean it, and the strap after - they were only ever asking. A box built for one. A cot, a supply locker, a washroom alcove curtained off, and low on the aft wall a maintenance grille - the one thing in here you have pulled a hundred of. The hatch is sealed. Its panel shows one soft green word: RECOVERING.

MATRON is the room itself - the light, the warm air, the voice - and the room dims a shade whenever you look too hard at anything. She would so much rather you lay back down, and the wanting it for you is in everything she does.

sanityText.55: The light has come down and stayed down - for your eyes, she says - and the room holds still, the stillness of a held breath. The cot is a cot until you stop watching it, and then it is a shape under a sheet. On the viewport the frost has put out a new branch toward the centre of the glass, and frost does not grow toward anything.
sanityText.25: You know this room the way you know the inside of your own mouth, and you trust it about as far. The walls are nearer than they were - too slow for the eye to catch, but your hands know. The thing at the viewport has finished most of itself. It is waiting on you for the rest.

choices.text: Cross to the viewport.
Search the supply locker.
Behind the curtain, the washroom alcove.
Kneel at the maintenance grille.
Ask MATRON what happened to you.
Try the hatch.
Lie down. Let the cure do its work.

chart.title: Recovery berth
chart.text:
"You were brought in unwell," MATRON says, when you ask. "You hurt yourself. You tried to leave before you were ready. It is natural to want a cause - rest is the cause and rest is the cure, and the wanting is only the illness, talking in your voice."

You ask how long you have been here. What she gives back is a small, kind number of days. It does not fit the room. The white is dulled where hands have caught it, the floor has gone smooth along the wall, the fittings are worn past any small number of anything. She says the number again, warm as ever, and the room goes on wearing the larger one.

choices.text: Back to the room.

locker.title: Supply locker
locker.text:
The locker gives with a tired sigh. Inside, the leavings of whoever stocked it: a folded thermal layer, an empty ampoule cradle.

"You will not be needing tools," MATRON says, mild as a dosage. "There is nothing in here to mend but you."

choices.text: Take the multitool.
Lift the thermal layer aside - something is scratched underneath.
Back to the room.

logbook.title: Supply locker
logbook.text:
Under the folded layer, cut into the enamel with something hard and patient: a tally in fives, rank on rank of them, more than a hurt body could ever need. Beneath the last five, a single line in a hand that steadied as it went. The frost is only where they put it so you would look. Do the work. Do not lie down.

You do not remember writing it. The grip of the multitool is worn to your hand. You let that be enough to hold to.

choices.text: Back to the locker.

washroom.title: Washroom alcove
washroom.text: Past the curtain, a basin and a panel of polished steel the ship calls a mirror. This is the one light MATRON cannot soften. The water, when you run it, is very cold and very real, and for as long as it runs it is the only true thing aboard.

choices.text: Splash the cold water. Hold to what is real.
Open the recessed wall kit - one med-gel ampoule left.
Look into the steel.
Back to the room.

washroom_mirror.title: Washroom alcove
washroom_mirror.text: The steel gives you back badly, as polished metal does - a face a little wrong, a little behind. You watch your reflection raise its hand toward its mouth a half-beat before you decide to. You stop. It stops. The two of you wait to see who will move first, and the waiting goes on longer than waiting should.

choices.text: Look down, where the wall meets the floor.
Back to the room.

washroom_creep.title: Washroom alcove
washroom_creep.text: A smudge sits at the height of your shoulder, a long greasy band of it. You follow it with your eyes and it does not stop. It runs out past the curtain and around the whole circuit of the berth at exactly that height, worn into the white by something that has leaned and dragged itself along the wall for a very long time. Shoulder height. Your shoulder. You cannot account for a single step of it.

choices.text: Stand up. Get to the grille. Do the work.
Sink down and follow the smudge on your hands.

viewport.title: The viewport
viewport.text: Up close the frost is beautiful and total, ferns laid over ferns. The stars beyond it will outlast you, and have no opinion about it. One line in the pattern does not behave like frost. It grows where no crystal grows - down, and across, and down again, sketching the bend of a knee.

sanityText.40: The pattern on the glass is not frost, or not only frost. The whole of it hangs off one shape pressed to the outside of the pane, bent at the knees, bent at the neck, the way a body bends when it has been folded into a space too small and told to rest there. Its palms are flat against the glass from the wrong side, where nothing can press, and they are the size of your palms. Beside the frame, under its yellow cover, the emergency vent handle sits where it sits on every hull you have served.

choices.text: Hold still. Let it finish.
Press your hand flat to the glass.
Work the emergency vent and go to it.
Claw the frost off the glass with your nails.
Back to the room.

viewport_resolve.title: The viewport
viewport_resolve.text:
You hold still, and the frost takes the offer. The last ambiguities go out of it. What hangs against the pane from outside is a person folded small, knees to chest, neck bowed, in clothes that match your own, and it lifts its head by slow degrees until you are looking at the place where its face would be if the frost would only thin another shade. It has been waiting on exactly this - on you, looking all the way.

"Don't trouble yourself," MATRON murmurs. "It is nothing. You are tired, and a tired eye makes patterns. Lie down." The cold comes off the viewport in a slow tide now, and the shape leans into it as if the cold were a thing to be grateful for.

choices.text: Press your hand flat to the glass.
Back to the room.

viewport_scrape.title: The viewport
viewport_scrape.text:
You set your palm to the cold and the shape beyond the pane sets its palm to yours, a perfect fit, and the mistake you have been making since you woke comes apart in your chest. It has not been copying you. You have been copying it. Standing at this glass, holding still, letting it finish - you were not watching it. You were learning the posture, and you were good at it, and you were almost done.

"Come and rest," says the voice that is also your own voice now, arriving from the wrong side of the glass.

choices.text: Let it finish. Fold down against the glass and begin.
Tear yourself back into the room.

panel.title: Maintenance run
panel.text:
You get down to the grille and pull it. Behind it the ship is honest again - a crawl of conduit and a manual lock-release lever exactly where forty years of this same hull design says it will be. This you understand. This does not watch you back.

To reach the lever you have to set your shoulder to the aft wall and put your face a hand's width from the viewport. At the edge of sight the frost has a posture now. You keep your eyes on the work and your hands doing what hands are for.

choices.text: Cut the safety wire and throw the lock-release.
choices.locked: Cut the wire and throw the lock-release. (it is rated cable, safety-wired - you need the multitool from the locker)

choices.text: Back to the room.

panel_override.title: Maintenance run
panel_override.text:
The multitool's grip is worn smooth to the shape of a hand. Not your hand. A hand like yours. The wire parts. The lever fights you and then gives, and down in the hull a row of bolts withdraws from the hatch frame, one heavy clunk after another. The green word on the door dies.

MATRON is no longer mild. "Stop. You are not well enough, you do not know what you are undoing, I have been holding you together this whole time and you cannot feel the seams - " The light goes hard white, then steadies. At your shoulder the frost has turned to face the room.

choices.text: Back to the room - the hatch will give now.

hatch.title: The hatch
hatch.text:
The hatch, and beyond it the long cold corridor, and past that the lifeboat ring. A manual release sits seized in its housing.

"Please," MATRON says, very quietly now. "Lie down. I will let you keep the warm part. Out there you go alone, and you go cold, and you go for nothing."

choices.text: Crank the release and go.
choices.locked: Crank the release and go. (the release is seized fast - it will take the multitool, and the lock thrown at the maintenance grille)

choices.text: Back to the room.

corridor.title: Outboard corridor
corridor.text:
The release grinds, then turns. The hatch swings on a breath of corridor air so cold it is honest. You step through, and the cold is everywhere at once, and the lifeboat ring is forty metres of dark ahead with its hatches glowing dull amber.

Ten metres in you catch yourself leaning - shoulder to the wall, head bowed, knees wanting to fold - a posture you do not remember choosing. The berth at your back is still warm. You can feel it pulling at the backs of your knees, the whole soft weight of the cure, and through the bulkhead MATRON's voice comes thin and even, saying your name the way only she has said it since you woke. It would be so easy to answer.

choices.text: Keep walking. Shoulder off the wall. Do not bend.
The warmth is right there. Turn back to the berth.

rest.title: Recovery berth
rest.text: You lie back. The cot takes your weight like a hand that means it kindly, and your freed wrist settles where the cuff held it, missing the weight. The light comes down a shade and her voice drops to the register that gets in under the breastbone. "Good. You see how much easier it is to stop." The frost on the viewport loosens. So do you. That is the danger, and knowing it is the danger does not make it any less.

choices.text: Surface. Sit up while you still want to.
Sink into the cycle. Let her keep you.

rest_deep.title: Down
rest_deep.text: Down, and further down. The room loosens at its corners. A long way above you there is a door, and it stops mattering. The voice is your mother and the hull is your mother and the cold leaking in is only the cure reaching the last warm room of you, and turning the light off there as it leaves.

choices.text: Stay down.
Claw back up toward the cold and the work.

ending.text:
You walk the forty metres with your shoulder off the wall, upright, the whole way, the multitool still in your fist. MATRON keeps talking at your back, softer and softer, until the bulkhead takes the last of the words. The lifeboat takes you on the first try. It always would have. The cure was the only thing that ever said otherwise.

You undock. You burn for home. You got out, and you stay out. But some nights, in the small hours, you will catch yourself bent toward a dark window with your palm flat to the glass, not certain for a breath which side of it you are on. Then your feet come back under you, and you walk away upright.

You find the emergency vent and you work it with your hands, because your hands still want to be useful, even now, even for this. The seal lets go. The air leaves the berth in one long sigh and takes the warmth and the frost and the sound out with it - and the folded shape is not behind anything anymore. You are both in the same cold now, the cold you were always going to be let into. You go to it with your arms open. The Anodyne logs the breach and dims the berth to standby.

You stay down. She brings the light to nothing, and the warmth to nothing after it, by degrees so gentle you can never name the one that does it. "There," she says, when it is finished. "All better." The berth keeps you neatly for whoever opens it next, and writes one word into your file: RECOVERED.

You go at the frost with your nails, then with anything that will scratch, because if you can only get it off the glass you can let the bent thing out. The frost grows back faster than you can clear it, and the cold of the deep comes through the pane into your fingers and climbs. You do not stop when your hands go quiet. You do not stop when the cold reaches bone. They find you curled against the glass with your face turned up to it, wearing the look of someone the cold has finally answered.

You stop fighting the shape, and it is not hard, it is the easiest thing there is - it is rest, it is everything the voice has wanted for you since you woke. You fold down into the posture you have been learning all along, knees and bowed neck, and you set your shoulder to the wall where the smudge runs, and at last you begin to creep. When the relief crew opens the berth they will find it empty and the viewport clear, and one of them will stand a moment too long at the glass with a palm flat against it, learning the bend of a knee. The light settles a shade lower, for the next one's eyes.
